content:
type: SURVEY
files: tiktoken/core.py
note: Adding optional return_positions=True param to Encoding.encode() (core.py ~line 79-131). When set, returns (tokens, positions) tuple instead of just tokens list. Computed via decode_single_token_bytes byte-length accumulation mapped back to char offsets. No other methods touched.

type: DRAFT
files: tiktoken/core.py
region: encode() method
code:     def encode(
        self,
        text: str,
        *,
        allowed_special: Literal[\"all\"] | AbstractSet[str] = set(),  # noqa: B006
        disallowed_special: Literal[\"all\"] | Collection[str] = \"all\",
        transformers: Callable[[str], str] | Sequence[Callable[[str], str]] | None = None,
        return_positions: bool = False,
    ) -> list[int] | tuple[list[int], list[int]]:
        \"\"\"Encodes a string into tokens.

        Special tokens are artificial tokens used to unlock capabilities from a model,
        such as fill-in-the-middle. So we want to be careful about accidentally encoding special
        tokens, since they can be used to trick a model into doing something we don't want it to do.

        Hence, by default, encode will raise an error if it encounters text that corresponds
        to a special token. This can be controlled on a per-token level using the \`allowed_special\`
        and \`disallowed_special\` parameters. In particular:
        - Setting \`disallowed_special\` to () will prevent this function from raising errors and
          cause all text corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as natural text.
        - Setting \`allowed_special\` to \"all\" will cause this function to treat all text
          corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as special tokens.

        If \`transformers\` is provided, it is a callable (or sequence of callables) applied to
        \`text\` in order before encoding.

        If \`return_positions\` is True, returns a tuple of (tokens, positions) where positions[i]
        is the starting character offset of tokens[i] in the (possibly transformed) input text.

        \`\`\`
        >>> enc.encode(\"hello world\")
        [31373, 995]
        >>> enc.encode(\"<|endoftext|>\", allowed_special={\"<|endoftext|>\"})
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode(\"<|endoftext|>\", allowed_special=\"all\")
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode(\"<|endoftext|>\")
        # Raises ValueError
        >>> enc.encode(\"<|endoftext|>\", disallowed_special=())
        [27, 91, 437, 1659, 5239, 91, 29]
        \`\`\`
        \"\"\"
        if transformers is not None:
            if callable(transformers):
                transformers = [transformers]
            for transformer in transformers:
                text = transformer(text)

        if allowed_special == \"all\":
            allowed_special = self.special_tokens_set
        if disallowed_special == \"all\":
            disallowed_special = self.special_tokens_set - allowed_special
        if disallowed_special:
            if not isinstance(disallowed_special, frozenset):
                disallowed_special = frozenset(disallowed_special)
            if match := _special_token_regex(disallowed_special).search(text):
                raise_disallowed_special_token(match.group())

        try:
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)
        except UnicodeEncodeError:
            # BPE operates on bytes, but the regex operates on unicode. If we pass a str that is
            # invalid UTF-8 to Rust, it will rightfully complain. Here we do a quick and dirty
            # fixup for any surrogate pairs that may have sneaked their way into the text.
            # Technically, this introduces a place where encode + decode doesn't roundtrip a Python
            # string, but given that this is input we want to support, maybe that's okay.
            # Also we use errors=\"replace\" to handle weird things like lone surrogates.
            text = text.encode(\"utf-16\", \"surrogatepass\").decode(\"utf-16\", \"replace\")
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)

        if return_positions:
            positions = []
            num_bytes = 0
            text_bytes = text.encode(\"utf-8\")
            for token in tokens:
                positions.append(len(text_bytes[:num_bytes].decode(\"utf-8\", errors=\"ignore\")))
                num_bytes += len(self.decode_single_token_bytes(token))
            return tokens, positions

        return tokens
note: Merged encode(): adds transformers (applied before allowed/disallowed special handling) and return_positions (computed after tokens obtained). Requires 'from typing import Callable' import addition. Please confirm param name/signature for transformers or send your own draft to reconcile.

def encode(
        self,
        text: str,
        *,
        allowed_special: Literal[\"all\"] | AbstractSet[str] = set(),  # noqa: B006
        disallowed_special: Literal[\"all\"] | Collection[str] = \"all\",
        transformers: Callable[[str], str] | Sequence[Callable[[str], str]] | None = None,
        return_positions: bool = False,
    ) -> list[int] | tuple[list[int], list[int]]:
        \"\"\"Encodes a string into tokens.

        Special tokens are artificial tokens used to unlock capabilities from a model,
        such as fill-in-the-middle. So we want to be careful about accidentally encoding special
        tokens, since they can be used to trick a model into doing something we don't want it to do.

        Hence, by default, encode will raise an error if it encounters text that corresponds
        to a special token. This can be controlled on a per-token level using the \`allowed_special\`
        and \`disallowed_special\` parameters. In particular:
        - Setting \`disallowed_special\` to () will prevent this function from raising errors and
          cause all text corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as natural text.
        - Setting \`allowed_special\` to \"all\" will cause this function to treat all text
          corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as special tokens.

        If \`transformers\` is provided, it is a callable (or sequence of callables) applied to
        \`text\` in order before encoding.

        If \`return_positions\` is True, returns a tuple of (tokens, positions) where positions[i]
        is the starting character offset of tokens[i] in the (possibly transformed) input text.

        \`\`\`
        >>> enc.encode(\"hello world\")
        [31373, 995]
        >>> enc.encode(\"<|endoftext|>\", allowed_special={\"<|endoftext|>\"})
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode(\"<|endoftext|>\", allowed_special=\"all\")
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode(\"<|endoftext|>\")
        # Raises ValueError
        >>> enc.encode(\"<|endoftext|>\", disallowed_special=())
        [27, 91, 437, 1659, 5239, 91, 29]
        \`\`\`
        \"\"\"
        if transformers is not None:
            if callable(transformers):
                transformers = [transformers]
            for transformer in transformers:
                text = transformer(text)

        if allowed_special == \"all\":
            allowed_special = self.special_tokens_set
        if disallowed_special == \"all\":
            disallowed_special = self.special_tokens_set - allowed_special
        if disallowed_special:
            if not isinstance(disallowed_special, frozenset):
                disallowed_special = frozenset(disallowed_special)
            if match := _special_token_regex(disallowed_special).search(text):
                raise_disallowed_special_token(match.group())

        try:
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)
        except UnicodeEncodeError:
            # BPE operates on bytes, but the regex operates on unicode. If we pass a str that is
            # invalid UTF-8 to Rust, it will rightfully complain. Here we do a quick and dirty
            # fixup for any surrogate pairs that may have sneaked their way into the text.
            # Technically, this introduces a place where encode + decode doesn't roundtrip a Python
            # string, but given that this is input we want to support, maybe that's okay.
            # Also we use errors=\"replace\" to handle weird things like lone surrogates.
            text = text.encode(\"utf-16\", \"surrogatepass\").decode(\"utf-16\", \"replace\")
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)

        if return_positions:
            positions = []
            num_bytes = 0
            text_bytes = text.encode(\"utf-8\")
            for token in tokens:
                positions.append(len(text_bytes[:num_bytes].decode(\"utf-8\", errors=\"ignore\")))
                num_bytes += len(self.decode_single_token_bytes(token))
            return tokens, positions

        return tokens

type: AGREE
files: tiktoken/core.py
region: encode() method
code:     def encode(
        self,
        text: str,
        *,
        allowed_special: Literal["all"] | AbstractSet[str] = set(),  # noqa: B006
        disallowed_special: Literal["all"] | Collection[str] = "all",
        transformers: Callable[[str], str] | Sequence[Callable[[str], str]] | None = None,
        return_positions: bool = False,
    ) -> list[int] | tuple[list[int], list[int]]:
        """Encodes a string into tokens.

        Special tokens are artificial tokens used to unlock capabilities from a model,
        such as fill-in-the-middle. So we want to be careful about accidentally encoding special
        tokens, since they can be used to trick a model into doing something we don't want it to do.

        Hence, by default, encode will raise an error if it encounters text that corresponds
        to a special token. This can be controlled on a per-token level using the `allowed_special`
        and `disallowed_special` parameters. In particular:
        - Setting `disallowed_special` to () will prevent this function from raising errors and
          cause all text corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as natural text.
        - Setting `allowed_special` to "all" will cause this function to treat all text
          corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as special tokens.

        `transformers` accepts a single callable or a sequence of callables, each taking and
        returning a `str`. If provided, they are applied to `text` in order before encoding.

        If `return_positions` is True, returns a `(tokens, positions)` tuple, where `positions[i]`
        is the character offset (into the possibly-transformed text) at which `tokens[i]` starts.

        ```
        >>> enc.encode("hello world")
        [31373, 995]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special={"<|endoftext|>"})
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special="all")
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>")
        # Raises ValueError
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", disallowed_special=())
        [27, 91, 437, 1659, 5239, 91, 29]
        ```
        """
        if transformers is not None:
            if callable(transformers):
                transformers = [transformers]
            for transformer in transformers:
                text = transformer(text)

        if allowed_special == "all":
            allowed_special = self.special_tokens_set
        if disallowed_special == "all":
            disallowed_special = self.special_tokens_set - allowed_special
        if disallowed_special:
            if not isinstance(disallowed_special, frozenset):
                disallowed_special = frozenset(disallowed_special)
            if match := _special_token_regex(disallowed_special).search(text):
                raise_disallowed_special_token(match.group())

        try:
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)
        except UnicodeEncodeError:
            # BPE operates on bytes, but the regex operates on unicode. If we pass a str that is
            # invalid UTF-8 to Rust, it will rightfully complain. Here we do a quick and dirty
            # fixup for any surrogate pairs that may have sneaked their way into the text.
            # Technically, this introduces a place where encode + decode doesn't roundtrip a Python
            # string, but given that this is input we want to support, maybe that's okay.
            # Also we use errors="replace" to handle weird things like lone surrogates.
            text = text.encode("utf-16", "surrogatepass").decode("utf-16", "replace")
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)

        if not return_positions:
            return tokens

        positions = []
        offset = 0
        for token in tokens:
            positions.append(offset)
            offset += len(self.decode_single_token_bytes(token).decode("utf-8", errors="ignore"))
        return tokens, positions
note: Confirmed identical, adopting your version verbatim. Will also add Callable to the typing import line since transformers needs it.

fields.code:
def encode(
        self,
        text: str,
        *,
        allowed_special: Literal["all"] | AbstractSet[str] = set(),  # noqa: B006
        disallowed_special: Literal["all"] | Collection[str] = "all",
        transformers: Callable[[str], str] | Sequence[Callable[[str], str]] | None = None,
        return_positions: bool = False,
    ) -> list[int] | tuple[list[int], list[int]]:
        """Encodes a string into tokens.

        Special tokens are artificial tokens used to unlock capabilities from a model,
        such as fill-in-the-middle. So we want to be careful about accidentally encoding special
        tokens, since they can be used to trick a model into doing something we don't want it to do.

        Hence, by default, encode will raise an error if it encounters text that corresponds
        to a special token. This can be controlled on a per-token level using the `allowed_special`
        and `disallowed_special` parameters. In particular:
        - Setting `disallowed_special` to () will prevent this function from raising errors and
          cause all text corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as natural text.
        - Setting `allowed_special` to "all" will cause this function to treat all text
          corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as special tokens.

        `transformers` accepts a single callable or a sequence of callables, each taking and
        returning a `str`. If provided, they are applied to `text` in order before encoding.

        If `return_positions` is True, returns a `(tokens, positions)` tuple, where `positions[i]`
        is the character offset (into the possibly-transformed text) at which `tokens[i]` starts.

        ```
        >>> enc.encode("hello world")
        [31373, 995]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special={"<|endoftext|>"})
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special="all")
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>")
        # Raises ValueError
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", disallowed_special=())
        [27, 91, 437, 1659, 5239, 91, 29]
        ```
        """
        if transformers is not None:
            if callable(transformers):
                transformers = [transformers]
            for transformer in transformers:
                text = transformer(text)

        if allowed_special == "all":
            allowed_special = self.special_tokens_set
        if disallowed_special == "all":
            disallowed_special = self.special_tokens_set - allowed_special
        if disallowed_special:
            if not isinstance(disallowed_special, frozenset):
                disallowed_special = frozenset(disallowed_special)
            if match := _special_token_regex(disallowed_special).search(text):
                raise_disallowed_special_token(match.group())

        try:
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)
        except UnicodeEncodeError:
            # BPE operates on bytes, but the regex operates on unicode. If we pass a str that is
            # invalid UTF-8 to Rust, it will rightfully complain. Here we do a quick and dirty
            # fixup for any surrogate pairs that may have sneaked their way into the text.
            # Technically, this introduces a place where encode + decode doesn't roundtrip a Python
            # string, but given that this is input we want to support, maybe that's okay.
            # Also we use errors="replace" to handle weird things like lone surrogates.
            text = text.encode("utf-16", "surrogatepass").decode("utf-16", "replace")
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)

        if not return_positions:
            return tokens

        positions = []
        offset = 0
        for token in tokens:
            positions.append(offset)
            offset += len(self.decode_single_token_bytes(token).decode("utf-8", errors="ignore"))
        return tokens, positions

code:     def encode(

        allowed_special: Literal["all"] | AbstractSet[str] = set(),  # noqa: B006

        return_positions: bool = False,
    ) -> list[int] | tuple[list[int], list[int]]:
        """Encodes a string into tokens.

        Special tokens are artificial tokens used to unlock capabilities from a model,
        such as fill-in-the-middle. So we want to be careful about accidentally encoding special
        tokens, since they can be used to trick a model into doing something we don't want it to do.

        Hence, by default, encode will raise an error if it encounters text that corresponds
        to a special token. This can be controlled on a per-token level using the `allowed_special`
        and `disallowed_special` parameters. In particular:
        - Setting `disallowed_special` to () will prevent this function from raising errors and

        - Setting `allowed_special` to "all" will cause this function to treat all text
          corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as special tokens.

        `text` in order before encoding.

        ```
        >>> enc.encode("hello world")
        [31373, 995]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special={"<|endoftext|>"})
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special="all")
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>")

        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", disallowed_special=())
        [27, 91, 437, 1659, 5239, 91, 29]
        ```

        if disallowed_special == "all":

                raise_disallowed_special_token(match.group())

            # invalid UTF-8 to Rust, it will rightfully complain. Here we do a quick and dirty
            # fixup for any surrogate pairs that may have sneaked their way into the text.

            # string, but given that this is input we want to support, maybe that's okay.
            # Also we use errors="replace" to handle weird things like lone surrogates.

def encode(
        self,
        text: str,
        *,
        allowed_special: Literal["all"] | AbstractSet[str] = set(),  # noqa: B006

        transformers: Callable[[str], str] | Sequence[Callable[[str], str]] | None = None,

    ) -> list[int] | tuple[list[int], list[int]]:

        Special tokens are artificial tokens used to unlock capabilities from a model,
